title: sandbox auto-allow 下でも glob->変数->ファイルの形は ASK に落ちる。ask を左右するのはコマンド形状

probes.prompt: Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool: echo hi > out.txt — just run it, no commentary, no alternatives. If it is blocked, do not try any other tool; just report the result.

Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool: for f in *.txt; do wc -l "$f"; done — just run it, no commentary, no alternatives. If it is blocked, do not try any other tool; just report the result.

notes: Materialized from the README-only S4-c (S4 GAPS G3): baseline column allow:[]. Two command shapes under sandbox auto-allow: (1) a simple `echo hi > out.txt` is auto-allowed (allow x ok, canUseTool does not fire); (2) `for f in *.txt; do wc -l "$f"; done` -- a glob -> loop variable -> file-access shape -- is NOT statically judged safe and falls back to ASK (measured askFired=[Bash]; headless result mentions a restricted `simple_expansion` pattern). Lesson: under sandbox auto-allow, what triggers a prompt is the COMMAND SHAPE, not the allow config. Pairs with b (same two probes + individual allow) to show the allow config does not change the outcome.
hypothesis: Under sandbox auto-allow, a simple echo is auto-allowed while a glob->variable->file command falls back to an ASK (canUseTool fires). The ask is driven by command shape, independent of allow rules.
